title: Runtime Configuration, Secrets, Environment Precedence, and Sensitive Data · Docker Build Room
summary: A platform pairing session must produce the first working configuration and secret-flow contract for runtime configuration, secrets, environment precedence, and sensitive data, with predictions recorded before each simulated build or lifecycle change. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of configuration and secret-flow contract while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence…
objectives: Separate build arguments, build secrets, image environment, runtime environment, Compose interpolation, configs, and application configuration.
Resolve CLI, Compose, env_file, image, shell, quoting, unset, empty, required, default, and literal-dollar environment behavior.
Grant only named services access to declared secrets and configs and verify file path, permissions, rotation, and local Compose limitations.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.config:
# docker-basics cumulative container delivery portfolio
# Browser labs perform deterministic manifest and evidence review only.
# They never connect to a Docker daemon, mount a socket, or execute learner commands.
workspace: docker
version-baseline:
  engine: 29.6.1
  compose: 5.3.1
  buildx: 0.35.0
  buildkit: 0.31.1
  dockerfile-frontend: 1.25
transfer-boundary: authorized-disposable-environment
evidence:

steps.title: Commit to the mission model · Workshop Docker Build Runtime Config Boundary
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop build receives credentials through ARG and ENV; Compose interpolation prints them; image history and logs retain copies. Classify public config, build secrets, runtime secrets, redaction, and rotation. Competency probe: Separate build arguments, build secrets, image environment, runtime environment, Compose interpolation, configs, and application configuration.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: Commit to the mission model · Workshop Docker Build Runtime Config Boundary.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for separate build arguments, build secrets, image environment, runtime environment, compose interpolation, configs, and application configuration. For Commit to the mission model · Workshop Docker Build Runtime Config Boundary, keep Docker Build Runtime Config Boundary connected to mechanically reviewable Docker artifact and transfer evidence.

content.label: Authority to consult after committing

steps.title: Extend the cumulative artifact · Workshop Docker Credential Helper Rotation
steps.instruction:
Append the Docker evidence item headed "  # Evidence: docker-credential-helper-rotation-17rmtr". Tailor its configuration-boundary.yaml change to the current stakeholder case, retain a deterministic review command, reject one concrete failure, and record a repair. The browser analyzes text only; run Docker commands later in an authorized disposable transfer environment.

Evidence target: Extend the cumulative artifact · Workshop Docker Credential Helper Rotation.
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns Docker Credential Helper Rotation into observable performance evidence. For Extend the cumulative artifact · Workshop Docker Credential Helper Rotation, keep Docker Credential Helper Rotation connected to mechanically reviewable Docker artifact and transfer evidence.

content.code:
# Evidence: docker-credential-helper-rotation-17rmtr
  - competency: docker-credential-helper-rotation
    artifact: configuration-boundary.yaml
    decision: credential helper rotation
    scenario-variant: 2
    environment: IPv6-enabled Compose project on a shared CI runner
    fault-injection: deny outbound DNS after initial service discovery
    retained-proof: accessible recovery runbook with rollback rehearsal timing
    case-key: 17rmtr
    change: |
      public-config: immutable-environment-map
      runtime-secret: mounted-file
      build-secret: buildkit-secret-mount
      logs: redact-values
      rotation: restart-with-versioned-reference
    verify: docker compose config --environment && docker image history --no-trunc candidate
    failure: Reject secrets in Dockerfile ENV, ARG, Compose interpolation output, image history, repository files, or diagnostic logs.
    repair: Separate public config from secrets, inject each at the narrowest lifetime, scan metadata, rotate the credential, and verify redaction.
content.caption: Shape of the required increment; replace generic language with the current stakeholder case.

content.title: Evidence is more than syntax
content.text: The structural check is only one gate. The following changed-case decision and explanation must also hold.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Use scoped tokens, credential stores or helpers, non-interactive CI login, logout, revocation, and rotation without command-line leakage.

hints: For Defend the transferred model · Workshop Docker Credential Helper Rotation, name the daemon authority, immutable identity, process, storage, network, secret, and cleanup boundary, Docker Credential Helper Rotation precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Defend the transferred model · Workshop Docker Credential Helper Rotation, trace this concrete input and predict build, image, container, mount, network, Compose, policy, and teardown evidence before reviewing it: A workshop build receives credentials through ARG and ENV; Compose interpolation prints them; image history and logs retain copies. Classify public config, build secrets, runtime secrets, redaction, and rotation. Competency probe: Use scoped tokens, credential stores or helpers, non-interactive CI login, logout, revocation, and rotation without command-line leakage.

For Defend the transferred model · Workshop Docker Credential Helper Rotation, challenge the documented misconception with manifest, inspect, lifecycle, failure, repair, and transfer evidence: docker login credentials are always stored encrypted and cannot leak through process or CI output.
